Upcoming Events Request an Audience
Who We Are

A Brotherhood Forged in Spirit

It began, as so many great institutions do, with a humble young man, a worthy son, and a singular conviction: that whiskey deserved more than to be consumed — it deserved to be celebrated. From the crossroads of Terrebonne Drive and County Road X in Wausau, Wisconsin, the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin was willed into existence in the year of our Lord 2019.

What followed was the assembly of 24 gentleman connoisseurs united not merely by a love of the dram, but by the deeper truths whiskey unlocks — camaraderie, candor, laughter, and the occasional drunken haiku.

"In the water of life, there is truth."

— Motto of the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin

Our society year runs from the eve of the NFL Draft to the eve of the next — a year measured not in calendars, but in drams savored and moments seized. The Society convenes in sport coat (preferably tweed), in fellowship, and in the pursuit of whiskey wisdom.

Whiskey procured for the gatherings of the Society shall only be savored during our illustrious meetings. Members are entitled to 1.5-ounce pours from each bottle present. The revered elixir may be consumed neat, on the rocks, or with water, as the drinker sees fit. "Whiskey" shall encompass any variety deemed appropriate by the board — including whiskey, whisky, bourbon, and Scotch.

All club directors are required to don a sport coat (preferably tweed) at any meeting. Newly initiated members must wear a button-down shirt and tie. First-time offenders will be mocked, ridiculed, and required to purchase a bottle of whiskey for the membership. Second-time offenders may be stripped of rank and possibly expelled at the Commodore's discretion.

Members must be at least 21 years of age, act responsibly during and after meetings, and abstain from whiskey snobbery. Members absent from a meeting forfeit their whiskey portion to those in attendance. Membership privileges last the duration of the Society year — from the first round of the NFL Draft to the eve of the next.

24 Members
2019 Founded
1.5 oz per pour
∞ Drams of Truth
The Officers
The Commodore
Presides over meetings, sets long & short term goals, guides the Society
Captain of Education
Manages all educational programs and meeting schedules
Captain of Public Relations
Manages the Society's image, communications, and website
Chief Secretary
Manages all record-keeping and correspondence

Disciplinary Record

Wall of Shame

The Society is not without compassion. But the Society is not without memory. Herein are recorded those whose conduct — or conspicuous lack thereof — required formal proceedings.

Acquitted
Jeffrey Schuster
Chronic AWOL · Case No. 001
The first member in Society history to face formal charges — and the first to be saved by a vote of his peers. Accused of chronic absenteeism and serial ditching of club functions, Schuster stood trial before the full membership. The verdict: acquitted by the slimmest of margins, carried across the finish line by a combination of personal charm, creative excuses, and what one member described as "weapons-grade bullshit." An OG member who perpetually hangs on by the thinnest of threads, Schuster remains a man of contradictions — unreliable at the bar, yet undeniably formidable at the billiards table, where his prowess in 3-ball commands genuine, if grudging, respect.
Awaiting Trial
[Name Withheld]
Wardrobe Violation · First Offense
Appeared at a Society gathering without sport coat. Was duly mocked and ridiculed per Article III. One bottle of whiskey paid as penance. Currently on probation pending further wardrobe decisions.
Acquitted
[Name Withheld]
Alleged Whiskey Snobbery · Dismissed
Charges of whiskey snobbery brought in violation of Article V. After review, the Council determined the accused was merely "enthusiastic." Case dismissed. The accused is reminded that the line between enthusiasm and snobbery is thin and watched closely.
Member Spotlight

In the Spotlight

Member Spotlight
Vice Commodore

Matthew Gelhar

Vice Commodore & Co-Founding Member · Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin

Every great institution has its origin story, and the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin is no different. At the crossroads of Terrebonne Drive and County Road X, where all good things begin, it was Matthew Gelhar — junior kin, co-conspirator, and the second signature on the sacred charter — who helped breathe life into what would become one of Wausau's most distinguished brotherhoods.

As Vice Commodore, Matthew occupies a singular and somewhat mythological position within the Society. He does not attend many gatherings. He has not attended most gatherings. There are newer members who have never laid eyes on the man, and who have quietly begun to wonder whether he exists at all — whether "Matthew Gelhar" is perhaps an honorific, a legend, a ghost who haunts the bylaws.

He is not a ghost. He is a founder. And that distinction, within this Society, is everything. His chair remains set at every meeting. His pour remains accounted for. And should he ever choose to grace us with his presence, the Society will rise — glasses raised — as one.

His dram of choice, when he deigns to appear: Lagavulin 16 — smoke, depth, and a finish that lingers long after the glass is empty. Much like the man himself.

Title Vice Commodore
Status Co-Founding Member
Preferred Dram Lagavulin 16yr
Member Since The Very Beginning
Appearances Rare. Cherished. Disputed.
Origin Wausau, Wisconsin
A Word From the Founder

The Commodore's Corner

The Commodore

Peter Gelhar

Commodore & Founding Member · Est. 2019 · Wausau, Wisconsin

Friends, gentlemen, and honored guests —

Welcome to the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin.

I'll be honest with you — and honesty, much like a fine dram, is something we hold in high regard here. This whole thing started as an idea. One of many, if you ask anyone who knows me. I have ideas the way Wisconsin has winters: frequently, with varying degrees of severity, and occasionally leaving those around me wondering what exactly they've gotten themselves into.

This one, though. This one worked.

What began in 2019 at the crossroads of Terrebonne Drive and County Road X — with a bottle, a charter, and a handful of gentlemen willing to take a chance on something — has grown into a brotherhood I am genuinely proud of. Twenty-four members strong. A motto that means something. Events that people actually show up to. Even a website, apparently.

We are not a drinking club. We are a Society. There is a difference, and it matters to us. We gather to learn, to share, to argue respectfully about whether Islay or Speyside deserves the crown, and to remind ourselves that the best moments in life are the ones shared with good people over something worth savoring.

So whether you're a member in good standing, a guest of a member, or simply someone who stumbled upon this page through some mysterious alignment of fate and search algorithms — you are welcome here. Pour yourself something worthy. Stay awhile.

In Aqua Vitae Veritas,

Peter Gelhar
Commodore, Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin

Upcoming Events

Annual Tournament
The Great Northern Open
I
Date TBD — Summer 2025
Greenwood Hills Country Club, Wausau

A pilgrimage for the discerning golfer. A symphony of swings and putts on the hallowed grounds of Greenwood Hills. To win the jacket is to etch one's name in the annals of the Society's history.

Inaugural Tournament
Grand Croquet Classic & Ladies Lawn Beautification Tournament
II
Mid-May 2025 · 1:00–5:00 PM
Greenwood Hills Country Club

Round robin croquet, knockout finals, Best Dressed Lady award, and a secret induction ceremony. Come in your croquet whites. Approximately $100 per couple.

Monthly Gathering
Society Meeting & Tasting
III
Scheduled per membership availability
Location TBD

Sport coats on. Glasses up. The Society convenes to taste, deliberate, and elevate one another's whiskey wisdom. 1.5oz pours per bottle. Drunken haikus optional but encouraged.

Triple Crown · Leg III
The Curling Classic
IV
Winter 2025–26
Wausau, Wisconsin

The third leg of the PWSOW Triple Crown. Rocks, brooms, ice, and whiskey. Membership induction rights may be earned on the ice.

Induction Ceremony
V
As warranted
The Sacred Grounds, Wausau

The Oath is taken. The jacket is presented — one that probably won't fit. New members are welcomed into the ranks with open arms and glasses raised. So let it be written, so let it be drunken.

The Society year begins with the first round of the NFL Draft and concludes on its eve one year hence. A new chapter opens. Membership dues renew. May the drams flow.

The Society Cellar

Recommended Expressions

A curated selection from the Society's collective palate. Rated on the PWSOW Dram Scale: from Regrettable to Transcendent.

Glenfarclas 12yr
Single Malt Scotch · Speyside
Rating
Transcendent

Rich and full-bodied. Dried fruit, sherry sweetness, a subtle hint of smoke. The Commodore's personal expression. To sip Glenfarclas is to understand why we gather.

NoseDried fruit, sherry, oak
PalateRich malt, raisins, gentle smoke
FinishLong, warming, spiced
ServeNeat, room temperature
Glenmorangie 10yr
Single Malt Scotch · Highland
Rating
Distinguished

Elegant and delicate. Fruity aromas, honey, a touch of citrus zest. Light and bright. The gateway dram for the newly inducted.

NosePeach, citrus blossom, honey
PalateVanilla, fruit, light malt
FinishClean, medium, sweet
ServeNeat or a drop of water
Lagavulin 16yr
Single Malt Scotch · Islay
Rating
Transcendent

Deeply smoky, peaty, and medicinal. Not for the uninitiated. But for those who have crossed the threshold — there is nothing finer on a cold Wisconsin night.

NosePeat smoke, seaweed, iodine
PalateDark fruit, smoke, coffee
FinishEnormously long, drying peat
ServeNeat, period
Buffalo Trace
Kentucky Straight Bourbon
Rating
Distinguished

The bourbon that converted Scotch men. Vanilla, caramel, a hint of mint. An outstanding value that has earned its place at many a Society tasting.

NoseVanilla, mint, molasses
PalateBrown sugar, caramel, spice
FinishLong, smooth, oak
ServeNeat or one large ice cube
Highland Park 12yr
Single Malt Scotch · Orkney
Rating
Distinguished

The great bridge between Highland and Islay. Heather honey, gentle peat, and a backbone of Viking resolve. The Society's recommendation for those seeking balance.

NoseHeather honey, soft peat
PalateOrange zest, smoke, malt
FinishMedium, warming, sweet smoke
ServeNeat or a few drops of water
Woodford Reserve
Kentucky Straight Bourbon
Rating
Respectable

Approachable, well-crafted, and handsome on the shelf. Cherry, tobacco, and spiced oak. An excellent companion to a Society meeting when the Scotch runs thin.

NoseCherry, tobacco, dried fruit
PalateRich caramel, spice, oak
FinishLong, spiced, warming
ServeNeat or with a rock
Petitions for Membership

Request an Audience

The Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin does not recruit. We discover. If you find yourself reading these words, it is possible — however remotely — that you may be of sufficient character to warrant consideration.

To be inducted, you must either associate with very special people, demonstrate your prowess in sport, or simply bide your time and make an impression upon a member in good standing.

  • Must be at least 21 years of age
  • Must act responsibly during and after meetings
  • Must abstain from whiskey snobbery, absolutely and always
  • Must be willing to wear a button-down and tie at first induction
  • Must respond to communications in a timely manner
  • Must carry on the illusion of liking whiskey, even if you don't

Petition Received

Your petition has been noted by the Chief Secretary and shall be reviewed by the Council of Elders at their earliest convenience. Should your application warrant consideration, you will be contacted by a member in good standing.

Do not hold your breath. But do not despair. The Society moves at its own pace, like a well-aged single malt.

Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin

© 2025 Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin · Wausau, WI · Est. 2019 · Coming to a state or country near you

Bob Hearn

Member · Oshkosh Chapter · Loyal Foot Soldier · Supper Club Connoisseur

There is a moment every new member knows. The moment before the jacket. The moment when the Society looms large and mysterious, when the bylaws feel weighty, when the assembled brotherhood of distinguished gentlemen seems like a lot to walk into. Bob Hearn had that moment. Bob Hearn moved through that moment. And what Bob found on the other side was, as it turns out, exactly his kind of people.

Brought into the fold by the Oshkosh Chapter and personally sponsored by none other than Gregory Gelhar — elder statesman, keeper of the Commodore's lodging, man of the immaculate lawn — Bob arrived with the quiet uncertainty of a man who wasn't entirely sure what he'd signed up for. The anxiety was understandable. The Society can be a lot to take in all at once. The jackets, the ceremonies, the proceedings, the wheel, the drunken haikus. It's a full experience.

But Bob settled in. Quickly. Naturally. With the ease of a man who, once he finds his people, is simply and completely at home. That is not a small thing. That is character revealing itself in real time.

As a loyal foot soldier to sponsor Greg Gelhar, Bob has embraced his role with admirable dedication — carrying the Oshkosh Chapter's banner with pride while ensuring that his patron's legacy is upheld, his lawn metaphorically tended, and his reputation fiercely protected. Greg chose well. The Society noticed.

On the golf course, Bob is quietly dangerous — the kind of player whose game sneaks up on you right around the time you've already lost. Don't be fooled by the affable demeanor. The man can play.

And when the round is done and the sun begins its descent over the Wisconsin horizon, Bob Hearn knows exactly where he wants to be — seated at his favorite supper club, knife in hand, facing down a prime rib of such aged, magnificent, perfectly prepared excellence that the rest of the day simply melts away. This is not a man who eats carelessly. This is a man who understands that a great meal, like a great dram, deserves to be approached with intention and savored without apology.

Bob Hearn is new to the Society. His future here is bright. The membership is richer for his presence, the Oshkosh Chapter stands a little taller, and somewhere Greg Gelhar is nodding with the quiet satisfaction of a man who knew exactly what he was doing.

Welcome, Bob. The jacket fits.

Randy Fisher

Member · Original Member · Keeper of the Homeland · Patron Saint of the Relocation Bar

Every great institution needs a home. A place where the ideas are born, the glasses are raised, and the founding documents — however informal — are signed in the spirit of good company and late night enthusiasm. The Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin found its home because of Randy Fisher. Whether Randy fully consented to this arrangement in real time is, at this point, a matter of historical debate. It was late. The NFL Draft had just concluded. The founder was persuasive. Randy said yes.

And with that yes, history was made.

The story goes that as the Society was being conceived at a post-draft gathering, Randy volunteered — or more accurately, graciously accepted the founder's enthusiastic vision — to open the doors of the Relocation Bar as the official home of this bold new brotherhood. The details of exactly how much volunteering was involved have been generously recharacterized over the years. What is not up for debate is what happened next. Randy delivered. Every single time.

This is the defining characteristic of Randy Fisher. He may not have leapt forward with unbridled enthusiasm. He may have surveyed the Society's more elaborate proceedings with the quiet restraint of a man who has seen things. But when the club needed something — anything — Randy never batted an eye. No fanfare, no negotiation, no conditions. Just a steady, reliable, unshakeable yes from a man who understood, perhaps better than anyone, what it meant to show up for people.

When the Great Northern Open needed a home for its after party, Randy opened the doors again. Because of course he did. That is who Randy Fisher is. The Society does not have to ask twice. Often it barely has to ask once.

Beyond his foundational role in the Society's origin story, Randy is a man of the outdoors in the most complete sense — an avid fisherman, hunter, and golfer who approaches each pursuit with the same quiet competence he brings to everything else. While others talk about getting out there, Randy is already out there, already doing it, already doing it well.

It should be noted — and noted with the full weight it deserves — that Randy's family is nothing short of royalty in the eyes of the Society. The Fisher name carries with it a warmth, a generosity, and a welcome that has meant more to this brotherhood than any trophy or ceremony could capture. The Society did not just gain a venue when Randy said yes that night. It gained a family.

Randy Fisher is OG whether he likes it or not. The jacket is non-negotiable. The legacy is already written.

Andrew Wagner

Member · Original Member · Architect of the Origin · Third in Line to the Throne

Before there was a Society, there was a curling sheet, a quiet man, and a very special bottle of whisky.

The year is lost to the warm haze of memory, but the moment is not. Andrew Wagner — with the unassuming grace that defines everything he does — extended a glass of Balvenie 14 Year Old Rum Cask to the man who would become the founder of the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin. The founder took a sip. The founder's world tilted on its axis. And somewhere in that silent, golden moment, everything that would become this Society — the jackets, the ceremonies, the curling disasters, the legal proceedings, the West Coast chapter, the Great Northern Open, the drunken haikus, all of it — was born.

Andrew Wagner did not found the Society. He did something more profound. He handed a man a glass and changed his life.

This is who Andrew is. Quietly instrumental. Understated in presence, immeasurable in impact. He does not fill the room with volume — he fills it with substance, with creativity, with the particular intelligence of a man who has thought carefully about everything before he speaks. When Andrew Wagner says something, you listen. Not because he demands it. Because he's earned it.

An Original Member in the truest sense — OG of OGs — Andrew has been there from the very beginning, present at the creation, a witness to and participant in every chapter of this Society's still-unfolding story. He has never once needed convincing. The crazy ideas? He's in. The ambitious schemes? Already thinking about how to make them better. The Society's most unhinged concepts have always found a willing and capable collaborator in Andrew Wagner.

And capable does not begin to cover it. Andrew is the Society's resident creator — the mind behind the magnificent, the hands that bring the improbable to life. It was Andrew who designed and built the Society's elegant Wheel of Fortune gambling spinner, a device of such craftsmanship and ingenuity that it stands as a physical monument to what this man brings to the table. He did not buy it. He did not commission it. He built it — because that is what Andrew Wagner does. He makes things. Important things. Beautiful things. Things the Society didn't know it needed until they existed.

Third in line to the throne, they say. A designation that sounds like a consolation prize until you understand that in this particular kingdom, third in line means you are the quiet architect behind the scenes — the one keeping the crown polished while everyone else is distracted by the ceremony. History has always been shaped by people like Andrew. The ones who hand someone a glass and set everything in motion.

The Society owes him a debt that cannot be repaid in whiskey alone. We try anyway.

Jerry Kronschnabel

Member · Great Northern Open Champion · Uncle Jer · The Breadman

There are members who sought out the Pop Whiskey Society. There are members who were recruited with great enthusiasm. And then there is Jerry Kronschnabel — a man who arrived reluctantly, surveyed the situation with the measured skepticism of someone who has seen things, and decided that begrudging participation was, on balance, better than missing out entirely. The Society has never been more grateful for a reluctant yes.

Jerry earned his jacket the old fashioned way — he won it. A past Great Northern Open Champion, Uncle Jer stepped onto the course, did what needed doing, and walked away with the most coveted garment in Wisconsin whiskey culture draped across his shoulders. No nepotism. No lingering. Pure performance. The man delivered.

But golf is merely one chapter in the legend of Jerry Kronschnabel. The deeper truth — the thing that elevates Jerry from distinguished member to Society treasure — is what happens when this man enters a kitchen. Swedish pancakes so good they constitute a spiritual experience. Pizza that reframes your entire relationship with the genre. Jerry does not cook for sustenance. Jerry cooks as a love language, and the Society has been on the receiving end of that love, and it is extraordinary.

Now, Jerry will also tell you — at length, with great conviction and a pride that borders on the evangelical — about the culinary traditions of the Upper Peninsula of Michigan. He speaks of pasties the way scholars speak of lost civilizations. With reverence. With authority. With the unshakeable certainty of a man who has never once entertained the possibility that his audience might be less than fully converted. We respect the passion. The pasties are fine. Swedish pancakes remain the headline.

Jerry is a generational member — his son stands proudly in the Society’s ranks alongside him, which says everything about the kind of man Jerry is and the kind of home he built. Legacy isn’t just about trophies. Sometimes it’s about raising someone worthy of the jacket.

One final matter. Jerry Kronschnabel is a foundling. We will not explain what that means. We will only tell you that it is one of the most fascinating facts in the Society’s membership roster, and that if you want to know more, you should ask Jerry directly. We dare you. We genuinely dare you.

Matthew Lange

Member · Inaugural Curling Champion, 2023 · Future Son-in-Law of the Founder

There are paths into the Pop Whiskey Society — distinguished service, sporting triumph, the slow accumulation of goodwill among the membership. And then there is the path walked by Matthew Lange, a journey so patient, so calculated, and so spectacularly well-executed that it deserves its own chapter in the Society’s lore.

Matt lingered. There is no other word for it. He existed in the Society’s orbit for what felt like a geological era — present enough to be known, absent enough to remain uninducted — biding his time with the quiet confidence of a man who understood that the right moment was coming. And when that moment arrived, he seized it with both hands.

In March of 2023, at the Society’s inaugural curling event, Matt Lange stepped onto the ice and did what Matt Lange does — he won. Convincingly. The membership had little choice but to acknowledge what had been obvious to everyone, including Matt, for quite some time. The jacket was presented. It probably fit.

Now, one might raise an eyebrow at the particular circumstances of Matt’s ascension. The whispers of nepotism are not entirely unfounded — he is, after all, marrying the founder’s daughter, a development that either speaks to remarkable romantic fortune or the most long-game Society play in recorded history. Possibly both. The membership has chosen to celebrate rather than investigate.

What cannot be questioned is Matt’s dedication. An avid golfer with a deep passion for F1 racing, Matt has demonstrated an almost preternatural ability to share the founder’s exact enthusiasms at exactly the right moments. Coincidence? Perhaps. Strategic genius? Almost certainly. Either way, the founder is happy, and a happy founder is a thriving Society.

Matt will be down for everything. Every event, every gathering, every adventure the Society conjures. He arrives ready, leaves satisfied, and somehow always manages to make the whole thing better simply by being there. He is the future of this Society — enthusiastic, capable, deeply invested in its success, and entirely motivated to keep things running smoothly for reasons that are both personal and deeply, beautifully self-serving.

The Society’s future is in great hands. Dingus made sure of it.

Pete Irwin

Member · Wausau Area Chapter · Great Northern Open Champion, 2024

There are athletes who train. There are competitors who win. And then there is Pete Irwin — who shows up dressed impeccably, picks up whatever game is being played, and proceeds to make everyone else look like they’ve never held a club, a mallet, a cue, or a horseshoe in their life.

Golf? Dangerous. Bocce? Lethal. Horseshoes? Don’t. Pool? Sit down. The man has apparently played everything once and mastered it quietly, which is somehow more unsettling than if he’d been practicing for years. In the boardroom and on the playing field alike, Pete operates with the calm, measured precision of someone who always knows exactly where this is going — and it’s going his way.

A proud member of the Wausau Area Chapter, Pete brings to the Society the full package: impeccable style, an unshakeable competitive spirit, and the rare ability to party with genuine enthusiasm while simultaneously training for a marathon. How these coexist is between Pete and whatever higher power governs such things.

In 2024, Pete etched his name permanently into Society lore by hoisting the Great Northern Open trophy — with his son by his side. It was, by all accounts, exactly the kind of moment the GNO was built for. Legacy, competition, family, and a jacket that actually fit.

An excellent and dedicated member. A champion. A man who will absolutely beat you at whatever you suggest next.

Travis Higgins

Member · Wausau Area Chapter

Every society has its foundation — the grand gestures, the legal minds, the coast captains, the elder statesmen. And then there is the quiet, indispensable force that holds it all together. Travis Higgins is that force.

A proud member of the Wausau Area Class, Travis has never needed convincing. When the Society calls, he answers. When there’s work to be done, he’s already rolled up his sleeves. When the room needs a lift, his presence is the lift. There is no agenda with Travis — no calculation, no condition. Just a genuinely good man showing up, every time.

His laissez-faire spirit is not indifference — it is mastery. The quiet confidence of a man who has figured out that positivity is not a posture, it’s a practice. In a world full of people looking for reasons to say no, Travis Higgins is a reliable, refreshing yes.

He hunts. Occasionally this pulls him from Society duties, and the membership accepts this with the understanding that a man who disappears into the Wisconsin wilderness and returns smiling is a man worth waiting for. Rumor has it he’s out there recruiting — because wherever Travis goes, people want to be around him. That’s not a coincidence. That’s character.

There’s a reason he is always welcome. There are actually many reasons. But you’ll understand the moment you meet him.

Rodney Handly

Member · Captain, West Coast Chapter (Seattle)

There are members who attend the party. Then there is Hot Rod — who is the party.

Rodney Handly, inducted with full honors at Annapolis, is the Pop Whiskey Society’s man on the West Coast and one of its most vital souls. As Captain of the Seattle Chapter, Rod carries the Society’s flag into the Pacific Northwest with the energy of a man who has somewhere to be and the grace of one who’s never in a hurry to leave.

His personal motto — it’s five o’clock somewhere — is less a saying and more a philosophy, a way of life, a constitutional amendment. Rod sleeps when he’s dead. Until then, there is whiskey to pour, parties to attend, and a time zone that refuses to cooperate with the rest of the membership. We don’t hold that against him.

A dedicated public servant, Rod represents the Society with pride, decorum, and an almost supernatural willingness to say yes. New whiskey? Yes. New adventure? Absolutely. Anything at all, really? Rod’s already there.

It should be noted — with both reverence and a touch of awe — that Rod survived the Great Curling Fall of 2023, an incident that claimed a not-insignificant portion of his recent memory and may or may not have involved 112 consecutive hours of consciousness. He persevered. The Society is better for it.

The West Coast timing is what it is. We are proud to call him brother.

Gregory Gelhar

Member · Elder Statesman · Official Lodging of the Commodore

Every great society has one — the older, wiser figure who joined reluctantly, surveyed the room with measured skepticism, and then never left. Gregory Gelhar is that man. Brother to the founders in spirit if not in caution, Greg arrived with reservations and departed with a jacket and a standing invitation.

A man of refined tastes, Greg’s passions are simple and non-negotiable: golf, a lawn so immaculately manicured it borders on the spiritual, and whiskey — always whiskey. He has been known to speak about his short game with the same reverence most men reserve for newborns and vintage Scotch.

Greg holds a distinction that few members can claim — he serves as the official lodging of the Commodore when Society business brings the founder to town. This act of hospitality alone has secured his legacy. The Society does not forget those who keep the Commodore comfortable and well-rested.

A word to the wise: Greg is unreachable on Sundays. Don’t ask why. Don’t test it. Simply respect it as you would a well-established house rule or a particularly peaty Islay malt — not for everyone, but beyond question.

One final note: Greg likes golf.

In Aqua Vitae Veritas.

Neil Doherty

Member · Permanent Chapter President, New York Area

There are men who bring whiskey to the table. Then there are men who bring whiskey, a legal brief, and the unshakeable confidence of someone born within earshot of the Atlantic Ocean. Neil Doherty is the latter.

Hailing from the greatest city on the planet — New York City — Neil carries with him the particular refinement that only comes from a life lived between Manhattan boardrooms and the boardwalk at Coney Island. Posh and gritty in equal measure, he is the Pop Whiskey Society’s answer to a question nobody thought to ask, and the answer turns out to be spectacular.

A legal wizard of the first order, Neil serves as the Society’s de facto counsel on matters ranging from the procedural to the profound — and everything in between. When the Society has needed clarity, contract, or sheer intellectual firepower, Neil has delivered. Think of him as our Robert Kardashian, except the verdict is always in favor of a good dram.

As Permanent Chapter President of the New York Area Chapter, Neil has carried the Society’s banner into the five boroughs and beyond, evangelizing the gospel of fine whiskey to anyone willing to listen — and a few who weren’t.

Do not mistake his polish for softness. This is a man who loves Coney Island. There’s grit under those cufflinks.

In Aqua Vitae Veritas

Matthew Gelhar

Vice Commodore · Co-Founding Member · The Junior Kin

Every great institution has its origin story, and the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin is no different. At the crossroads of Terrebonne Drive and County Road X, where all good things begin, it was Matthew Gelhar — junior kin, co-conspirator, and the second signature on the sacred charter — who helped breathe life into what would become one of Wausau's most distinguished brotherhoods.

As Vice Commodore, Matthew occupies a singular and somewhat mythological position within the Society. He does not attend many gatherings. He has not attended most gatherings. There are newer members who have never laid eyes on the man, and who have quietly begun to wonder whether he exists at all — whether "Matthew Gelhar" is perhaps an honorific, a legend, a ghost who haunts the bylaws.

He is not a ghost. He is a founder. And that distinction, within this Society, is everything. Matthew Gelhar's name is on the charter. His vision is woven into the very fabric of what we do, why we gather, and how we pour. The Society did not merely happen — it was built, and he was among those who laid the foundation. One does not question the architect simply because he has moved on to other pursuits.

His chair remains set at every meeting. His pour remains accounted for. And should he ever choose to grace us with his presence, the Society will rise — glasses raised — as one.

His dram of choice, when he deigns to appear: Lagavulin 16 — smoke, depth, and a finish that lingers long after the glass is empty. Much like the man himself.

Peter Gelhar

Commodore · Founding Member · The Man With The Ideas

Peter Gelhar has had a lot of ideas. A remarkable, some might say alarming, number of ideas. Business ventures, schemes, propositions, and grand plans have flowed from this man with the reliable frequency of a well-aged cask — and like most good whiskey, the results have varied. Some ideas have faded quietly into the night. Others have caused mild concern among friends and family. And then there is this one.

The Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin is, by any reasonable measure, Peter Gelhar's finest hour. Born in 2019 from equal parts genuine passion and characteristic audacity, the Society has grown into something that even its founder could not have fully anticipated — a brotherhood of 24 gentlemen bound by camaraderie, ceremony, and the shared conviction that whiskey deserves to be taken seriously, even when everything else does not.

As Commodore, Peter presides over all Society matters with the authority of a man who knows he built the thing and the humility of a man who knows it has long since outgrown him. He sets the agenda, pours the first dram, and reserves the right to declare any gathering officially adjourned. He is simultaneously the Society's greatest champion and its most enthusiastic participant — often the last one still standing at the end of the evening, glass in hand, already describing his next idea.

His whiskey of choice says everything you need to know: Balvenie Caribbean Cask 14 — a classic Speyside that spent its final years in rum casks, emerging with unexpected warmth, a touch of the exotic, and a sweetness that catches you slightly off guard.

It is, in short, the Peter Gelhar of whiskies.

James Baranowski

Loyalist · The Gatekeeper · Minister of Supply

There are members of the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin who attend the meetings. There are members who plan the meetings. And then there is James Baranowski — the man who makes the meetings worth attending, the room worth entering, and the evening worth remembering.

Jim is a liquor and beer salesman by trade, which means he didn't just join a whiskey society — he came home. While the rest of the membership pores over tasting notes and debates finish lengths, Jim is three calls ahead, quietly ensuring that whatever the Society desires will be procured, delivered, and priced in a manner that makes the Commodore smile. He is, in the truest sense of the word, connected.

But here is the thing about Jim that no title or job description can fully capture: the man knows everyone. Not in the casual, vaguely familiar way most people mean when they say that. Jim knows everyone the way a mayor knows a city — by name, by preference, by the story behind the story. Walk into any room in Wausau with James Baranowski and you will leave three hours later having met twelve people you didn't know and somehow feeling like old friends with all of them. He has a gift for people that is frankly almost unfair.

It is this quality — more than the access to rare bottles, more than the industry connections, more than the generous pours — that makes Jim irreplaceable to the Society. A brotherhood is only as strong as the bonds between its members, and Jim has always been the man who deepens those bonds. He remembers the things people say when they think no one is listening. He shows up. He makes the call.

A loyalist from the Society's earliest days, Jim has been present for every chapter — the triumphs, the questionable decisions, and the occasional gathering that shall not be named in polite company.

And when the question inevitably arises — as it always does, late in the evening, when someone spots something extraordinary on a shelf or in a catalogue and dares to wonder aloud — Jim answers before it's even fully asked.

The deal is never done. If Jim can't get it, no one can.

Jeffrey Schuster

OG Member · Billiards Savant · First Man Tried

Every society needs a character who tests its boundaries, and the Pop Whiskey Society of Wisconsin found its in Jeffrey Schuster. An original member from the earliest days, Schuster has occupied a peculiar and uniquely precarious position within the brotherhood — perpetually on the edge, perpetually one absence away from formal proceedings, and perpetually, somehow, still here.

Schuster holds the dubious distinction of being the first member in Society history to be brought up on formal charges. The accusation: chronic and flagrant absenteeism, a pattern of ditching club functions so reliable it had become something of a running joke — until it wasn't. The trial was real. The charges were read aloud. And when the votes were cast, Jeffrey Schuster was saved — barely — by the very peers he had spent years avoiding.

It is a testament to the man's singular gift that he survived at all. Jeffrey Schuster is, by universal and uncontested agreement, the bullshitter of the group. His stories are long. His excuses are elaborate. His capacity for spin would make a career politician blush. And yet — and this is the part that confounds everyone — it works. Not always. Not on everyone. But just enough, just often enough, to keep him in the fold.

Where Schuster commands genuine and unironic respect, however, is at the billiards table. His skill in 3-ball is the stuff of quiet legend within the Society — a domain where the bullshit falls away and something real emerges. At the table, Jeffrey Schuster is focused, formidable, and surprisingly graceful. It is, perhaps, the one arena where the man's reputation is built entirely on merit.

An OG member hanging on by the edge — but hanging on all the same. The Society wouldn't be the same without him, though it will never admit this to his face.
